title: Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
Share of GDP per capita spent by the government on each primary school student vs. GDP per capita, 2008
GDP per capita data is adjusted for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.

The scatter plot maps government expenditure per primary school student as a percentage of GDP per capita against the GDP per capita for various countries in 2008. The horizontal axis displays GDP per capita in international dollars on a logarithmic scale ranging from $2,000 to $100,000, while the vertical axis measures the expenditure share from 0% to over 40%. Points are colored by region, distinguishing nations in North America, South America, Africa, Europe, Asia, and Oceania. The distribution shows no clear linear relationship between national wealth and the relative share of that wealth spent on each student. Serbia occupies the highest position on the vertical axis, allocating over 40% of its GDP per capita to primary education. Djibouti follows with a share near 38%, while Albania and Ukraine both sit above the 30% mark. In contrast, Kazakhstan records the lowest visible value, falling below 5%. Other nations with low expenditure shares include Bermuda, Saint Kitts and Nevis, and Rwanda, all positioned near the bottom of the chart. Among high-income nations, Austria, Malta, and Australia cluster between 20% and 25%, while Cyprus and South Korea appear near 30%. Lower-income countries display a wide spread, with Lesotho and Honduras exceeding 20%, whereas Burundi, Niger, and the Democratic Republic of Congo remain below 15%. The data points for European nations generally occupy the middle-to-upper section of the vertical range, while African and Asian nations appear across the full spectrum of expenditure shares. The chart shows that the proportion of GDP per capita directed toward primary schooling varies independently of a country's overall economic output per person.
